hcd_data.statement_of_contribution: Each property has value as a nineteenth-century House-form building that reflects vernacular interpretations of Queen Anne Revival and other Victorian architectural styles. Through its construction date and typology, each property is associated with the District’s early residential development from the 1850s to the early 1900s. Constructed as a semi-detached pair, the properties retain most of their original building features including the shared roofline with central gable, and mirrored facades featuring brick cladding and decorative brickwork, flat headed and round arched window and door opening, and shingle clad turrets with decorative woodwork. Each property has contextual value because it supports and maintains the historic mixed use character of the area and is physically, functionally, visually, and historically linked to the surrounding market area. Summary: Reference Primary Address Entrance Address(es) Number 367. 13 St. Andrew Street N/A 369. 15 St. Andrew Street N/A 195 | FO :D FO 5202 14 St. Andrew Street

context.street_character: Two-way | 30 km/h | Wide roadway exceeds minimum | Short commercial node

photo_observations.facade_colour: dark red brick with green-shingled turret roofs and cream/stone trim
photo_observations.condition_notes: heritage building, some ground floor openings may be boarded, but turrets and brickwork intact
photo_observations.window_notes: arched ground floor openings (2 per unit visible on #13 side, some appear glazed, some possibly boarded), rectangular upper windows with flat stone lintels, small gable window
photo_observations.door_notes: central or side entry per unit
photo_observations.turret_notes: polygonal (hexagonal/octagonal) turrets with conical green-shingled roofs and decorative finials, one per unit (mirrored pair with #15)
photo_observations.decorative_elements: soldier courses, string courses, decorative brickwork, shingle-clad turrets with green paint/stain
photo_observations.fence: wooden picket fence at front with construction/storage materials visible
photo_observations.adjacent_features: colourful mural/graffiti wall visible on left side (adjacent building)
photo_observations.notes: EXCELLENT night photo. Victorian semi-detached pair (13 & 15 St Andrew), mirrored facades. 2.5 storeys plus twin turrets with conical green-shingled roofs. Large round arched ground-floor openings (4 total across both units). Shared central gable with small attic window.

facade_description: 13 St Andrew St presents a house-form, semi-detached facade within the Kensington Market HCD. The street elevation spans roughly 5.2 metres across 3 storeys and is capped by a cross-gable, consistent with its house-form, semi-detached typology. The facade reads primarily as brick, laid in running bond with light grey mortar joints. The facade keeps a domestic opening rhythm of 2, 2, 1 window bays from lower to upper levels, using mixed, and 1 principal entrance. Character-defining facade elements include turret, bay window, decorative brick, cornice. HCD note: Each property has value as a nineteenth-century House-form building that reflects vernacular interpretations of Queen Anne Revival and other Victorian architectural styles. Through its construction date and typology, each property is associated with the District’s early residential development from the 1850s to the early 1900s. Constructed as a semi-detached pair, the properties retain most of their original building features including the shared roofline with central gable, and mirrored facades featuring brick cladding and decorative brickwork, flat headed and round arched window and door opening, and shingle clad turrets with decorative woodwork. Each property has contextual value because it supports and maintains the historic mixed use character of the area and is physically, functionally, visually, and historically linked to the surrounding market area. Summary: Reference Primary Address Entrance Address(es) Number 367. 13 St. Andrew Street N/A 369. 15 St. Andrew Street N/A 195 | FO :D FO 5202 14 St. Andrew Street
